title: AMNC26: AD: US and China: From Here to Where?
gist: A WEF AMNC26 panel (Allison, Kraus, Ngai, Zhao Hai, moderated by Vinjamuri) reads the recent Trump-Xi summit as a modest but real stabilization of US-China ties toward 'constructive strategic stability,' while insisting the underlying Thucydidean structure still dominates. Panelists argue trade is reorienting to the rest of the world, that the US-China AI/tech rivalry may create two booming economies while leaving others behind, and that business must adapt to permanent ambiguity.

insights.claim: The US-China relationship is governed by a 'great contradiction' of two simultaneous imperatives: the fiercest great-power rivalry of all time and an inextricable entanglement that forces cooperation for mutual survival.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Investors should treat both decoupling narratives and full-cooperation narratives as incomplete; the base case is managed, unstable coexistence rather than resolution in either direction.
insights.quote: On the one hand, this is the fiercest, lucidity and rivalry of all times.
insights.locator: OK, so one of the great risks

insights.claim: Structural forces determine roughly 75% of the outcome in the US-China relationship, leaving only about 25% to leadership agency and diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: This quantified framing implies summit optimism is capped: even skilled statecraft can only bend a minority of the trajectory, so tail risk of confrontation stays structurally embedded.
insights.quote: the structural reality is 75% of what's happening
insights.locator: So to the question, was the summit for real?

insights.claim: In 12 of the past 16 historical cases where a rising power threatened to displace a ruling power, the result was war, making catastrophic conflict the 'history as usual' baseline rather than an aberration.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: The 12-of-16 base rate is a hard prior for pricing geopolitical tail risk; it argues against complacency even during periods of visible détente.
insights.quote: In the last 500 years, 12 of the 16 cases that we've seen ended in war
insights.locator: Right. And I think, again, part of the reason

insights.claim: Left to normal behaviors of countries, companies, and politicians, the relationship is likely to end in catastrophic war within the next generation.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: A named authority explicitly betting on generational-horizon war is a signal to maintain long-dated hedges against a US-China military rupture even amid near-term stabilization.
insights.quote: I would say in the next generation, it's likely that it ends in a catastrophic war.
insights.locator: So to the question, was the summit for real?

insights.claim: Mutual assured destruction, financial entanglement, and a shared biosphere mean an escalated US-China war could leave no living Chinese and no living Americans, making cooperation a survival requirement rather than a preference.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: The existential downside is the structural brake that keeps both leaders pulling back from the brink; it underpins the 'floor' beneath the relationship that stabilizes risk assets during crises.
insights.quote: at the end of that war, there could be no living Chinese in China and no living Americans
insights.locator: The other half of problem

insights.claim: China is projected to keep rising and, with four times the US population, should over time overtake the US in every arena if successful.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Framing China's ascent as a demographic-scaled structural inevitability supports long-run allocation to Chinese and Chinese-competitive assets despite cyclical political friction.
insights.quote: China's a meteoric rising power, and as far as I can see, it's going to continue to rise.
insights.locator: OK, so one of the great risks

themes.theme: The Trump-Xi summit and the roadmap to 'constructive strategic stability'

insights.claim: The two leaders reframed the relationship away from 'great power competition' toward 'constructive strategic stability,' a pointer rather than a fully filled-out blueprint to be developed over 2026.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: The new label signals both governments want a managed ceiling on hostility; watch whether subsequent summits add substance or leave it as rhetoric.
insights.quote: to a constructive strategic stability as a pointer, not all filled out
insights.locator: So many, many people were critical

insights.claim: Up to four Trump-Xi summits may occur in 2026 (Washington in September, APEC sidelines in November, and the G20 in Miami in December), an unprecedented cadence of leader-level engagement.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: A dense summit calendar creates recurring catalyst dates for sentiment and tariff/tech headlines; each is a potential volatility event for China-exposed equities.
insights.quote: So we may have four summits of these two leaders, something we haven't seen before.
insights.locator: So many, many people were critical

insights.claim: Xi opened the summit by asking whether the two countries can overcome the Thucydides trap, which the panel treats as a correct diagnosis of the core problem.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Xi framing the relationship in Thucydidean terms signals Beijing is consciously managing escalation risk, supporting the case that both sides seek a controlled equilibrium.
insights.quote: The question is, are we going to be able to overcome Thucydides' trap?
insights.locator: I was heartened by the summit

insights.claim: China views the summit as genuinely real because both sides issued statements recognizing a strategic-stability relationship, though whether it will be realized in practice remains the open second step.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Beijing's insistence on the summit's authenticity, paired with doubt on execution, tells investors the near-term stabilization is credible but implementation risk is the thing to monitor.
insights.quote: it's absolutely real, because both sides has put out statements recognising there's a strategic stability relationship
insights.locator: I would say, first of all

insights.claim: A key doubt about the summit is that Xi's three opening questions and his proposal of a constructive strategic-stability relationship reportedly went unanswered by Trump, raising skepticism about whether it was a real dialogue.
insights.speaker: Audience member
insights.so_what: The non-response critique is a caution that headline stabilization may lack reciprocal substance; a one-sided framework is fragile to the next US policy swing.
insights.quote: when President Xi started that three question, he was not responded
insights.locator: Thank you, Leslie, because you looked for the third time

insights.claim: The summit's main achievement was resuming supply-chain flows, exemplified by China again being able to obtain engines for its airplanes.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Concrete supply-chain reopening (aero engines) is a tradeable, verifiable de-escalation marker to track versus rhetoric; reversals would signal renewed decoupling.
insights.quote: now we can get engines for our aeroplanes
insights.locator: I think the key of yesterday's speech

themes.theme: The A-plus business delegation as strategic signal

insights.claim: Trump brought the most powerful business delegation ever assembled to any country, including Tim Cook, Elon Musk, Jensen Huang of Nvidia, and Larry Fink of BlackRock, which the panel reads as a deliberate symbolic signal.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: The caliber of CEOs signals US business wants continuity of China access; their presence is a bullish tell for firms most dependent on the Chinese supply chain and market.
insights.quote: This was the A plus that never has been a business delegation ever to anywhere of equivalent sort.
insights.locator: So this was not only a business delegation

insights.claim: US business leaders understand decoupling is far too simplistic because the Chinese industrial engine and supply chain are more efficient than anything else globally, and an Apple phone cannot exist in current form without it.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: The business elite's rejection of decoupling caps how far political pressure can sever supply chains; hard-decoupling bets against China manufacturing are likely mispriced.
insights.quote: the Apple phone, right cannot be... What it is today without the Chinese supply chain
insights.locator: You know, I think the business sector completely realised

insights.claim: Nvidia's Jensen Huang appears to have joined the delegation at the last minute from Alaska, possibly not communicated to Xi's side until late, with no chairs prepared for the group at the back of the room.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: The disorganized inclusion of Nvidia hints US semiconductor policy toward China remains improvised and contested inside the administration, a live risk for chip-export names.
insights.quote: Jumping on a plane from Alaska.
insights.locator: So how do you make sense of that?

insights.claim: In 2026 there have been more multinational CEO and board meetings held in China than the panelist has ever witnessed, signaling deepening rather than retreating corporate engagement.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: A surge in board-level China visits is a leading indicator that multinational capital is re-engaging, contrary to the 'uninvestable China' narrative of prior years.
insights.quote: This year, 2026, I've seen more multinational CEOs and boards having the meetings in China than I've ever seen.
insights.locator: If you go into... The contradiction in the business sector

themes.theme: Trump's unpredictability and the Xi personal relationship

insights.claim: Trump treats unpredictability and chaos as a strategy for creating opportunity, splitting his time between reality-TV producer instincts and consequential world leadership, sometimes blurring the two.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Deliberate chaos as method means policy discontinuity is a feature, not noise; volatility premia on US-China headlines are structurally elevated under this administration.
insights.quote: he thinks being unpredictable, even creating chaos creates opportunity
insights.locator: We all are, you know, things spin so quickly

insights.claim: Despite general inconsistency, Trump maintains a steady, admiring line toward Xi, having concluded after the 145% tariff and supply-chain confrontation that China must be treated differently as a peer power.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: A durable Trump-Xi personal channel is the single most stabilizing variable; its persistence supports the near-term détente thesis even as other US-China vectors deteriorate.
insights.quote: After the 145% tariffs and then the supply chain choking, there was kind of an awakening.
insights.locator: There's one relationship about which

insights.claim: Trump's retreat from the supply-chain confrontation followed warnings that Ford and F-35 producers would have to shut production lines within a month, showing he backed down to material industrial facts rather than pressure.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: US rare-earth/component dependence forced a policy reversal; this reveals China's coercive leverage over US defense and auto supply chains as a real, repeatable pressure point.
insights.quote: When he started getting phone calls from Ford Motor Company or from the F-35 producers in a month, we're going to have to close our production lines.
insights.locator: So many, many people were critical

insights.claim: The structure of US politics guarantees continuing suspicion and hostility toward China across Congress and the foreign-policy establishment, meaning most actors want to push competition without grasping the catastrophic downside.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Leader-level détente sits atop a hostile institutional base; any weakening of the Trump-Xi channel could quickly re-expose the relationship to hawkish congressional forces.
insights.quote: there's going to continue to be increasingly, in my view, a suspicion, hostility, and otherwise
insights.locator: No, I would say the structure gives you 75%

insights.claim: The upcoming September and later summits will coincide with the US midterms, meaning Trump's domestic political standing is likely to spill unpredictably into his China foreign policy.
insights.speaker: Leslie Vinjamuri
insights.so_what: Midterm dynamics inject a domestic-politics wildcard into the summit calendar; positioning should account for China policy being used as an electoral instrument in H2 2026.
insights.locator: Good luck, and good luck because those

themes.theme: Trade reorientation and the rest of the world

insights.claim: If the US and China stopped trading with each other entirely, global trade would fall only about 2%, undercutting the assumption that the bilateral relationship is the whole game.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: A ~2% global-trade sensitivity reframes US-China decoupling as regionally disruptive but globally survivable; diversified trade exposure is more resilient than headlines imply.
insights.quote: let's imagine US and China stop trading with each other altogether, zero. What happens to global trade? Answer, minus two percent.
insights.locator: So can I do one footnote

insights.claim: US-China bilateral trade is declining while China's overall trade is growing, as trade corridors boom across Latin America and Southeast Asia and China now has more trade corridors with more countries than ever.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: China is successfully rerouting trade away from the US; exposure to China-Global South corridors (LatAm, ASEAN) captures the growth the bilateral decline masks.
insights.quote: China has more trade corridors with more countries ever, right, in the last few years.
insights.locator: Better than it what it when China was uninvestable

insights.claim: The roughly 25% reduction in Chinese trade to the US shifted to Southeast Asia, as illustrated by soybean demand moving to Brazil.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: The soybean-to-Brazil shift is a concrete substitution signal; US agricultural exporters lose share while Brazilian and ASEAN suppliers capture displaced China demand.
insights.quote: look at what happened with soybeans in Brazil
insights.locator: Before we get to that

insights.claim: The S&P 500 rose about 16% last year while Chinese, Korean, Japanese, and Taiwanese markets returned multiples of that, contradicting the notion that the US equity market is uniquely strong.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Asian equity outperformance versus the S&P is an explicit steer toward rotating into Chinese, Korean, Japanese, and Taiwanese markets over US large-cap.
insights.quote: So S&P last year, about 16%... All of them, multiples of that. Multiples of that!
insights.locator: Number two, Joe said rightly

insights.claim: Most of the world is hedging between the US and China rather than choosing, with companies and governments redistributing supply chains and relationships as the rest of the world rises.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: Persistent third-party hedging sustains demand for 'China plus one' and diversified sourcing plays; supply-chain reconfiguration is a durable multi-year investment theme.
insights.locator: Before we get to that

themes.theme: AI and tech rivalry: makers, takers, and a two-economy boom

insights.claim: US-China tech rivalry could create the greatest boom in both economies over the next ten years, with US tech companies running ahead and Chinese firms dominating industrial sectors, while the rest of the world risks being left behind.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: The thesis argues rivalry is bullish for both US and Chinese tech leaders simultaneously; the underweight risk sits with ex-US/China markets like Europe.
insights.quote: this rivalry will create the greatest boom in their own economies for the next ten years
insights.locator: Of course, as business, right, we are looking at different opportunities

insights.claim: In AI and the frontiers of science and technology, the world splits into makers and takers, with only the US and China as makers, and any smartphone or AI model must ultimately come from one of the two.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: A US/China duopoly at the tech frontier means value accrues to the two makers; 'digital sovereignty' bets elsewhere face structural dependence on American or Chinese platforms.
insights.quote: there's makers and there's takers, and there's U.S. And China, and then there's others
insights.locator: Well, I think Joe said earlier

insights.claim: Over the past 20 years, missing tech meant missing out, and over the next 10 years that will be even more pronounced, with the biggest innovation and technology leadership concentrated in the US and China.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: Reinforces concentration of returns in US and Chinese tech; the opportunity cost of underweighting frontier tech is framed as rising, not falling.
insights.quote: If you have not been in tech, you have kind of missed out. In the next 10 years, it will be even more so than ever before.
insights.locator: Of course, as business, right, we are looking at different opportunities

insights.claim: Even as US and Chinese markets hit all-time highs, the AI-driven sprint could leave everyone else trapped, with Europe unable to keep pace and its rule-making arriving too late to matter at the frontier.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: The bearish read on European tech competitiveness argues for structural underweight of EU tech relative to US/China leaders in AI value capture.
insights.quote: are they so far sprinting ahead that leaves the reservoir behind?
insights.locator: Maybe we'll have NASDAQ all time high

insights.claim: Rather than only makers and takers, capital-poor countries are adapting US and Chinese AI to local markets and leapfrogging legacy infrastructure, gaining access to medical and other solutions they never had before.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: A counter-thesis that emerging markets are net beneficiaries of frontier AI diffusion points to applied-AI and healthcare-access opportunities in leapfrogging economies.
insights.locator: So if you use the makers and takers

themes.theme: China open for business and US investment friction

insights.claim: Premier Li's speech framed the moment as 'China opportunity 2.0, not China shock 2.1,' emphasizing that China is open for business.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Beijing's official positioning as an inbound opportunity rather than an export threat is a policy signal supporting renewed foreign direct investment into China.
insights.quote: that's why Premier Li-tao is talking about China opportunity 2.0, not China shock 2.1
insights.locator: I think the key of yesterday's speech

insights.claim: Chinese firms, especially in EVs, solar panels, and new energy, want to invest in the US but are blocked by regulatory uncertainty, as US governors defer to a federal investment board whose composition and rules remain undefined.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Persistent US screening friction blocks Chinese clean-energy FDI; the undefined federal investment board is a concrete regulatory catalyst to watch for inbound Chinese capital.
insights.quote: but now we have a board of investment. I don't know where does that go, who is going to be in those board
insights.locator: I think the key of yesterday's speech

insights.claim: Whereas last year China was framed as 'the problem,' this year it is either less of a problem or a partial solution, while the top US agenda items (affordability, others) are self-generated by Trump's own policies.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Reframing China as part of the solution to US inflation/affordability creates a policy rationale for tariff relief that would benefit China-linked consumer supply chains.
insights.quote: Last year, China is the problem. And this year, I think China is either less of a problem or be partial solution.
insights.locator: I think the key of yesterday's speech

insights.claim: The competitiveness barrier for foreign firms entering China is no longer geopolitics but whether they are good enough, since China's business ecosystem is 'the world's toughest gym' full of hungry, capable domestic competitors.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: Market access is no longer the constraint; foreign firms lacking a genuine competitive edge will lose to domestic Chinese rivals regardless of policy openness.
insights.quote: China business ecosystem being the world's toughest gym, and you come in here and these guys are hungry
insights.locator: I'll do a quick one, though

insights.claim: With US R&D cutbacks, a biopharma company cannot exist without being part of China's innovation ecosystem, which will define many future medicines.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: Names a specific sector, biopharma, where Chinese innovation dependence is becoming unavoidable, arguing for China R&D exposure in drug pipelines.
insights.quote: You cannot exist without trying to be part of an ecosystem that's going to define a lot of our medicines for the future.
insights.locator: Okay, so I think you have to bifurcate

themes.theme: Corporate strategy: resilience, multi-local, and living with ambiguity

insights.claim: The CEO's job has fundamentally changed: making the best product at the best price is no longer sufficient, and leaders must now navigate trade corridors, tariffs, and distribution geopolitics that were never in the equation before.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: Geopolitical navigation is now a core operating competency; firms that institutionalize supply-chain and trade-policy capability carry a durable moat over product-only competitors.
insights.quote: the way that I need to think about the world is no longer making the best product in my factory at the best price possible
insights.locator: Yeah, well, first of all, I think that being a CEO

insights.claim: Companies must shift from a 'multinational' one-size approach to a 'multi-local' strategy with far more granularity, appointing an internal 'Secretary of State' in each market to manage geopolitical relationships.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: The multi-local model favors firms with deep local political and regulatory capability per market; it raises the fixed cost of global operations, disadvantaging thin-footprint players.
insights.quote: We've also been talking to our clients in terms of being multi-local.
insights.locator: I was gonna have a quick parenthetical

insights.claim: Businesses have already adjusted to a new reality of permanent ambiguity and uncertainty, making resilience rather than clarity the operative goal for leaders.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: The market has largely repriced US-China uncertainty; the surprise risk is now to the upside on stabilization rather than the downside on shock.
insights.quote: businesses have already adjusted to the new reality
insights.locator: Yeah. Look, you know, I'm actually quite optimistic

insights.claim: Chinese companies are the most competitive against themselves, aggressively going overseas and competing with each other rather than presenting a unified national front.
insights.speaker: Joseph Luc Ngai
insights.so_what: Intra-Chinese competition abroad compresses margins and accelerates price wars in export sectors; foreign incumbents face not one Chinese rival but many undercutting each other.
insights.quote: Chinese companies are the most competitive with themselves.
insights.locator: I agree with that. I think businesses are very selfish animals.

insights.claim: Chinese firms enjoying open markets and overseas expansion want less government intervention, while only those under foreign-state pressure (notably in tech) lobby for protection and countermeasures.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Chinese corporate policy preferences are bifurcated; tech firms facing US decoupling will drive state countermeasures, a key channel for escalation in the semiconductor arena.
insights.locator: OK, I'll go first. I think yes and no

themes.theme: Middle powers, fragmentation, and third-party dynamics

insights.claim: A deteriorating and fragmenting international trading and investment system makes the biggest players stronger, so middle powers must unify into a larger market to generate the innovation and finance needed to compete.
insights.speaker: Zhao Hai
insights.so_what: Fragmentation rewards scale; the investment implication is consolidation among middle powers and continued dominance of the two largest blocs absent coordinated countervailing markets.
insights.quote: the bigger is getting stronger. Because you have a unified market
insights.locator: I think what we're seeing is the consequences

insights.claim: The US, once the architect and builder of the international order, has become its principal wrecking ball, as captured by the Munich Security Report's 'wrecking ball politics' framing.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: US role reversal from order-builder to disruptor raises the risk premium on US alliance reliability and institutions, a structural headwind for dollar-order-dependent assets.
insights.quote: the architect and builder of the international order has become the principal wrecking ball
insights.locator: On this one, I would say the Munich Security

insights.claim: European views of America's reliability as an ally have dropped dramatically, with G7 leaders visibly fearful of coming into the crosshairs of the great disruptor.
insights.speaker: Graham Allison
insights.so_what: Eroding transatlantic trust accelerates European strategic autonomy and defense spending, a tailwind for EU defense and sovereignty-related sectors.
insights.quote: all of the leaders were just... Fearful hoping that they would not come into the crosshairs of the great disruptor
insights.locator: On this one, I would say the Munich Security

insights.claim: China is likely conducting behind-the-scenes discussions to help resolve Middle East conflicts, positioning itself as a mediator.
insights.speaker: Margery Kraus
insights.so_what: China's expanding diplomatic mediation role in the Middle East signals rising geopolitical influence that could reshape energy and regional risk premia over time.
insights.quote: I would bet a lot of money that there are discussions behind the scenes. Where China has been involved in helping to fix the problem.
insights.locator: Thank you. I pick up on that too.

insights.claim: US-China agricultural cooperation is a live area of engagement, with a recent bilateral agriculture conference and proposals to reward farmers for ecosystem services, protect prime farmland, and grow soil carbon as a crop.
insights.speaker: Rattan Lal
insights.so_what: Agriculture is flagged as a concrete cooperation lane; payment-for-ecosystem-services and soil-carbon markets are niche themes to monitor if bilateral ag ties deepen.
insights.quote: grow carbon in land as a crop, so that farmers can get income out of it
insights.locator: When Mr. Trump was visiting China
